acceptance: 1. Probe loop scans providers in quota_exhausted state and probes any whose retry_after has elapsed (with a default fallback interval of 5 min when retry_after is unknown). 2. Successful probe transitions provider back to available. 3. Failed probe extends retry_after with bounded backoff. 4. Loop is cancellable via context; goroutine lifecycle is tied to service lifecycle. 5. Unit tests cover: elapsed retry_after triggers probe, success restores available, failure extends retry_after, fallback interval applied when retry_after unset. 6. Existing tests pass. 7. Depends on fizeau-92b4b823.
description: Add a periodic lightweight probe that, at each quota_exhausted provider's retry_after (or at a fallback interval, default 5 min, whichever is sooner), issues a HealthCheck to that provider. On success the provider transitions back to available; on continued failure the retry_after is extended (bounded backoff). Extends existing service_probe.go. Does not modify per-request dispatch behavior — other providers continue serving requests normally while quota_exhausted ones are excluded. Depends on the state machine in fizeau-92b4b823.
title: fizeau: periodic HealthCheck probe restores quota_exhausted providers

description:
Hard removal of the deprecated preset aliases introduced by agent-a365bcf2 (preset rename to intent-flavored names).

The rename of preset IDs from harness-flavored (agent / claude / codex / cursor / worker) to intent-flavored (default / smart / cheap / minimal / benchmark) introduced a deprecation period where the old names still resolve via prompt.DeprecatedPresetAliases, emit a stderr warning, and forward to the canonical preset. This bead is the hard-removal follow-up.

Trigger: one release after the rename ships. Downstream DocumentDrivenDX/ddx config files and any CI scripts using the old names MUST be updated first — otherwise this bead will break them.

Scope:
- Delete DeprecatedPresetAliases from prompt/presets.go
- Delete DeprecatedPresetNames() from prompt/presets.go
- Change ResolvePresetName to return a fatal error (or return the default with a louder warning — design decision at time of implementation) for unknown / deprecated names
- Remove the back-compat tests added in agent-a365bcf2 (TestResolvePresetName_DeprecatedAliases, TestResolvePresetWithWarning_DeprecatedCodexAliasEmitsWarning, TestResolvePresetWithWarning_DeprecatedAliasInConfig)
- Remove the Deprecated preset names section from README.md
- Remove the Deprecated harness-flavored names section from docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-003-system-prompts.md
- Remove the deprecated-alias paragraph from docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-provider-config.md
- CHANGELOG entry documenting the hard removal

Pre-flight check before starting this bead: grep DocumentDrivenDX/ddx repo for any reference to --preset (agent|claude|codex|cursor|worker) and confirm it has been migrated.
title: preset: remove deprecated harness-flavored preset aliases (agent, claude, codex, cursor, worker)

acceptance: 1. Tests prove higher-power prepaid frontier wins when effective marginal cost is low and quota is healthy. 2. Tests prove local/free candidates are selected over paid cloud when they satisfy requested power, tools, context, and availability constraints. 3. Tests prove local/free preference does not override MinPower or exact model/provider-source/endpoint/harness pins. 4. Tests prove no-power default routing chooses the best/cheapest viable auto-routable model from discovered inventory. 5. Tests prove cheaper/faster candidates break ties inside the requested power range. 6. Tests prove provider/deployment class power separation prevents benchmark-only local/community models from tying cloud frontier models. 7. Score components are included in RouteDecision candidate trace. 8. go test ./internal/routing ./... -run 'Score|Power|Cost|Quota|Latency|Local' passes or a documented narrower equivalent passes.
description: Implement the utility ranking for eligible automatic candidates after power filtering is correct. In-scope: routing score code and tests. Score should prefer sufficient/high power, prepaid/free/local effective cost when viable, lower marginal cost, healthy quota/reset state, availability, and speed/latency. Provider/deployment class is already folded into catalog power; scoring must not treat a local/community model as equivalent to cloud frontier simply because one benchmark is high. It must not widen hard pins or retry. Keep weights deterministic and expose score components for trace output.
notes:
Prework review: bead is complete but broader than the last two: it changes ranking policy and candidate trace output. It is testable through internal/routing score tests plus service/route candidate trace tests. Acceptance is concrete but weight-sensitive, so implementation should keep the scoring model deterministic and small rather than introducing config rules.

Improvements captured before implementation: write scenario tests before changing weights for (a) local/free sufficient-power beats paid cloud, (b) MinPower and hard pins still constrain selection, (c) prepaid frontier with healthy quota can win on high power/effective cost, (d) cheaper/faster tie-breaks inside a power range, and (e) score components expose power/cost/speed/quota inputs so callers can explain the decision.

Implementation notes: scorePolicy now uses catalog power for eligible candidates, applies bounded marginal-cost penalties only when power metadata is present, adds zero-cost/local/prepaid healthy-quota bonuses, and preserves speed as a small tie-breaker. Candidate traces now include power, speed, cost class, and quota inputs across internal candidates, public RouteCandidate components, routing-decision events, override auto-components, and route-status JSON/text. Tests cover prepaid frontier, sufficient local/free, MinPower and exact-pin constraints, cost/speed tie breaks, and component propagation.

Verification: go test ./internal/routing -run 'Score|Power|Cost|Quota|Latency|Local' -count=1; go test . ./cmd/agent ./internal/routing -run 'Score|Power|Cost|Quota|Latency|Local|RouteCandidate|RouteStatus|ResolveRoute|Override' -count=1; go test ./internal/routing ./... -run 'Score|Power|Cost|Quota|Latency|Local' -count=1; go test ./...
title: routing: score candidates by power cost speed and quota

acceptance:
1. RouteStatusReport has a RoutingQuality field of type RoutingQualityMetrics with auto_acceptance_rate, override_disagreement_rate (both float64), and override_class_breakdown ([]OverrideClassBucket). AST-checked.
2. UsageReport has a RoutingQuality field with the same metrics aggregated over the report window.
3. The existing per-(provider, model) success-rate UI is labeled 'provider reliability' (e.g. ProviderReliabilityRate or a docstring/comment); the conflation between routing quality and provider reliability is removed.
4. TestRoutingQualityMetricsAcceptanceRate verifies: 100 requests, 30 with override events -> auto_acceptance_rate = 0.70.
5. TestRoutingQualityMetricsDisagreementRate verifies: 30 overrides, 18 where pin != auto on any overridden axis -> override_disagreement_rate = 0.60. Coincidental-agreement overrides (12 of 30) pull the rate down.
6. TestRoutingQualityMetricsClassBreakdown verifies: feeding override events with mixed prompt_features and axes produces the expected pivot, including outcome aggregation (success/stalled/failed counts per bucket).
7. TestProviderReliabilityNotRenamedToRoutingQuality structural test asserts the legacy provider-reliability metric exists as a separate field, not folded into RoutingQuality.
8. go test ./... -count=1 passes.
description:
Bead 2 of ADR-006. Surface the three first-class routing-quality metrics on RouteStatus and UsageReport, distinct from per-(provider, model) reliability.

New metrics computed from override and rejected_override events emitted by bead 1 (agent-9fc2633c):
- auto_acceptance_rate = no-override requests / total requests (windowed)
- override_disagreement_rate = (overrides where pin != auto on at least one overridden axis) / overrides. Coincidental-agreement overrides are in the denominator but not the numerator.
- override_class_breakdown = pivot of (prompt_features bucket, axis_overridden, match_per_axis) -> count + outcome aggregates.

UI-level changes:
- Existing per-(provider, model) success-rate field on RouteStatusReport gets renamed or relabeled as 'provider reliability' / ProviderReliabilityRate. Stays mechanically the same.
- New RoutingQualityMetrics type containing the three new metrics, exposed on RouteStatusReport.
- UsageReport gains a RoutingQuality summary section (totals over the report window).

Both metrics need windowing — RouteStatus uses a recent-window (e.g. last 100 requests or last 24h), UsageReport uses the report's --since window.

Depends on agent-9fc2633c. The override events are the input data; bead 2 just aggregates them. If bead 1 isn't merged, bead 2's tests can use synthetic event injection.

In-scope files:
- service.go (RoutingQualityMetrics struct, RouteStatusReport extension, UsageReport extension)
- service_routestatus.go (computation from override events)
- service_usage_report.go or session/usage.go (windowed aggregation across session logs)
- service_routestatus_test.go and usage_report_test.go (metric correctness)

Out of scope:
- CLI surface for the breakdown (bead 3)
- Automatic learning driven by the metric (deferred to future ADR)
notes:
REVIEW:BLOCK

`service_session_projection.go:148` / `routingQualityForUsageWindow`: `UsageReport` must aggregate override and rejected_override events from the session logs selected by the report window, not from `service.routingQuality`. This misses historical logs, cross-restart reporting, and the bead’s requested UsageReport window semantics.
`service_session_projection.go:132`: when `publicSessionLogDir()` is empty, `UsageReport` returns `&UsageReport{}` before setting `RoutingQuality`, so even current in-memory routing-quality data is dropped for that path.
`service_routing_quality.go:306`: live records are captured before final outcome and never updated, so RouteStatus/UsageReport data sourced from the in-memory ring cannot contain real success/stalled/failed outcome aggregates. The UsageReport fix should consume persisted override events with outcomes from session logs.

Reopened after gpt-5.5 review on commit c13758f returned BLOCK with three real architectural defects (NOT environmental):

1. **service_session_projection.go:148 — UsageReport sources from in-memory ring instead of session logs.** routingQualityForUsageWindow reads service.routingQuality (live in-memory ring), but UsageReport semantics require windowed aggregation from session logs over --since DURATION. The in-memory ring is bounded and recent-only; it can't show historical, cross-restart, or window-specific data. RouteStatus may legitimately use the in-memory ring; UsageReport must scan session logs for override and rejected_override events within the window and aggregate from those.

2. **service_session_projection.go:132 — early return drops RoutingQuality.** When publicSessionLogDir() is empty, UsageReport returns &UsageReport{} before setting RoutingQuality, so even current in-memory routing-quality data is dropped for that path. The early return must happen AFTER the RoutingQuality field is populated, or the field must be set unconditionally before any return.

3. **service_routing_quality.go:306 — live records captured pre-final, never updated.** Records are captured at override-event emission (before final event) and never updated with the outcome. AC #6's outcome aggregation (success/stalled/failed counts per bucket) is fundamentally broken because outcome data never lands in the ring. Two paths to fix: (a) update the ring entry after the final event fires (back-write pattern), or (b) emit override-with-outcome only after final, defer the override event itself — but bead 1 AC #2 requires override event BEFORE final, so this isn't viable. Option (a) is the right approach.

Architectural note for the next attempt: RouteStatus and UsageReport have different windowing semantics. RouteStatus reports recent live state from the in-memory ring; UsageReport scans session logs over the requested window. Both should expose the same RoutingQualityMetrics shape, but their data sources differ. The override and rejected_override events written to session logs by bead 1 (9fc2633c) are the authoritative record for windowed/historical reporting. The in-memory ring is for fast-access operator-dashboard latency only.

Also: bead 1 AC #5 required outcome population post-execution. Verify bead 1's commit (622ab6c, 05de672) actually does this update; if not, that's a third bug surface that crosses both beads. The override event payload schema includes outcome.{status, cost_usd, duration_ms} — those fields must be populated by the time consumers read either the in-memory ring or the persisted log record.

REVIEW:BLOCK

BLOCK: `service_execute.go`, rejected override path: `makeRejectedOverrideEvent` is called and `s.hub.broadcastEvent(sessionID, rejectedEv)` runs at `service_execute.go:123-124`, but no `serviceSessionLog` is opened and no `writeOverrideEvent(ServiceEventTypeRejectedOverride, payload)` call occurs. Since `UsageReport` now reads routing quality from session logs via `service_session_projection.go:175-182`, rejected overrides are missing from report-window metrics whenever a log directory is configured.
BLOCK: `service_taillog.go:189-190` persists only `ServiceEventTypeOverride` in the final-event fan-out path. `service_session_log.go:126` supports writing `ServiceEventTypeRejectedOverride`, and `internal/session/routing_quality.go:64` scans it, but the implementation never writes those events on the pre-dispatch rejection path.

Reopened after gpt-5.5 review on commit 5b0f949 returned BLOCK with two related defects in the rejected_override persistence path:

1. **service_execute.go:123-124 broadcasts rejected_override via hub but never writes to session log.** makeRejectedOverrideEvent is called and s.hub.broadcastEvent(sessionID, rejectedEv) fires, but no serviceSessionLog is opened and no writeOverrideEvent(ServiceEventTypeRejectedOverride, payload) call occurs. Since the prior fix made UsageReport read from session logs (service_session_projection.go:175-182), rejected_override events are now invisible to UsageReport whenever a log directory is configured. The whole point of bead 2's window-aggregation fix is undermined for the rejection class.

2. **service_taillog.go:189-190 only persists ServiceEventTypeOverride.** The infrastructure exists for both: service_session_log.go:126 supports writing ServiceEventTypeRejectedOverride, internal/session/routing_quality.go:64 scans for it on read. But nothing writes them on the pre-dispatch rejection path. The persistent log loses the rejection event; the in-memory ring may also miss it depending on broadcast order.

Architectural note for the next attempt: rejected_override fires PRE-DISPATCH. Session log open today happens during dispatch initialization (service_execute.go later in the function). To persist the rejection event, the implementation needs one of:
(a) Open the session log briefly in the rejection branch — write the single rejected_override event, close it. Lightweight; mirrors the broadcast-then-persist pattern used for normal override events.
(b) Move session log open earlier (at request-validation time, before the rejection branch) so both override and rejected_override paths share the same persistence call.
(c) Persist rejected events to a separate path (not tied to session ID). Heavier but cleaner separation.

Lean toward (a) — least architectural churn, mirrors the existing override-event flow. (b) tightens invariants but requires care that session-log open doesn't re-fail in ways that mask the original rejection error. (c) is over-engineered for this round.

Whichever path: the resulting log entries must be readable by routing_quality.go:64's existing scanner so UsageReport over the --since window picks up rejected_override count alongside override count. Verify with TestRoutingQualityMetrics_RejectedOverrideAggregatedFromSessionLogs (or equivalent name) that constructs a session log fixture with N rejected_override entries and asserts UsageReport counts them correctly.
title: metrics: separate routing-quality from provider-reliability on RouteStatus and UsageReport

description:
Codex is temporarily explicit-only for automatic routing because full baseline certification is not complete. Before re-enabling AutoRoutingEligible, codex routing must consume durable Codex quota cache/probe state instead of hard-blocking non-Claude subscription harnesses, and the primary baseline doc/report must point to evidence for request controls, model discovery, reasoning, usage input/output/total tokens, cancellation/error status, permissions, workdir, and quota freshness.

In-scope files are expected to include internal/harnesses/codex/, service_routing.go, routing/service tests, and docs/helix/02-design/primary-harness-capability-baseline.md.

Out-of-scope: weakening the automatic-routing eligibility gate or promoting other secondary harnesses.
notes: Follow-up hardening: Codex quota cache now persists non-secret account metadata from auth.json; automatic routing requires a fresh quota snapshot plus a subsidized ChatGPT account plan. Missing account, API-key-only auth, stale quota, empty windows, or blocked windows keep Codex out of automatic routing.
title: harness-codex: restore auto-routing after quota-aware baseline certification

acceptance: Automated CLI/integration tests prove agent resolves --model-ref through the catalog end-to-end; external manifest override behavior is covered; deprecated refs are rejected by default and accepted with --allow-deprecated-model; tests are deterministic and CI-safe.

5. Integration test (optional but recommended): run against a real omlx endpoint or a test double that emulates omlx's silent-termination behavior when thinking is present. Assert the request completes without 'unexpected end of JSON input'.

description:
## Summary

The agent library sends an Anthropic-specific `thinking` field in OpenAI-compatible request bodies to non-Anthropic providers (lmstudio, omlx, openrouter, ollama). Providers that accept and parse the request then silently terminate their SSE stream after the first delta frame when their generation pipeline encounters the unknown field. Downstream consumers see `unexpected end of JSON input`.

This bug was classified via wire capture using the new AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE=1 flag (this repo's agent-941e7e42, landed in v0.3.12). Evidence was gathered from DocumentDrivenDX/ddx ddx-6a5dfe35.

## Wire evidence (v0.3.12, 2026-04-18)

```
POST http://vidar:1235/v1/chat/completions
Content-Type: application/json
Body includes:
  "stream":true,
  "stream_options":{"include_usage":true},
  "thinking":{"budget_tokens":8192,"type":"enabled"},
  "tools":[... 6 tools ...],
  "model":"Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit"
```

Response:
- HTTP 200
- Content-Type: text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
- Body captured: 186 bytes total
  - Frame 1: `: keep-alive\n\n` (SSE keepalive)
  - Frame 2: valid `data: {..."delta":{"role":"assistant"}}\n\n` (assistant role start)
  - Then: stream terminates. No `data: [DONE]`. No error frame. Just EOF.

`thinking` is Anthropic's extended-thinking API. It is NOT in the OpenAI chat-completions schema. vidar-omlx (OpenAI-compatible, no Anthropic semantics) has no handler for it and its generation pipeline fails silently mid-stream.

Reproduced on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit AND Qwen3-Coder-Next-MLX-4bit — model-independent. Reproduced across two projects (ddx repo + agent repo) — project-independent. Reproduced on v0.3.11 and v0.3.12 — both versions exhibit the bug.

## Root cause

The openai-compat provider's request-serialization path does not gate flavor-specific fields. `thinking` is serialized into the wire body unconditionally when the caller has passed the equivalent Anthropic-style configuration (via preset or CLI flag). Since v0.3.12 now has Provider.DetectedFlavor() (agent-92f0f324), the library has the information it needs to gate this — it just doesn't use it in the serializer.

## Fix direction

In the openai-compat provider's Chat/ChatStream request builder:

1. Before writing the request body, check `p.DetectedFlavor()`.
2. If the flavor is NOT one that supports Anthropic thinking semantics (i.e., NOT `anthropic-passthrough` or similar — in practice all current openai-compat flavors: lmstudio, omlx, openrouter, ollama, plus `openai-native`), omit the `thinking` field from the serialized JSON.
3. Emit a debug log (at Trace level or via AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE when that flag is on) noting that `thinking` was stripped for flavor X. Operators should see this when diagnosing 'why isn't my local model using thinking'.

The anthropic provider package continues to include the field in its own serializer (no change there).

Long term, consider a broader per-flavor field allowlist/denylist in the openai-compat serializer — `thinking` is probably not the only Anthropic-specific field that leaks. Audit the request builder for other non-portable fields.

## Alternative (less preferred)

Reject at config-load: if Config passes `thinking` config AND any provider in the same config has a non-Anthropic DetectedFlavor, emit a startup warning. Does not help when providers are added at runtime and does not handle the case where a single agent run targets multiple providers with different flavors in the same session.

Stripping at serialization is cleaner.

## Other non-portable fields likely to have the same issue

These are candidates for the same per-flavor gate:
- `metadata` with Anthropic-specific keys
- `system` as a top-level field vs in messages array (OpenAI uses messages[].role=system; some providers may tolerate both, some may not)
- `anthropic_version` or similar API-version fields
- Extended-thinking budget controls beyond `thinking`

Audit as part of the fix; file follow-up beads if other fields exhibit the same symptom.

## Related

- agent-92f0f324 (v0.3.12): DetectedFlavor() — this fix consumes it
- agent-941e7e42 (v0.3.12): AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE — this bug was classified using it
- agent-767549c7 (v0.3.12): capability flags — related but orthogonal (capability gating wouldn't have prevented this specific leak)
- DocumentDrivenDX/ddx ddx-6a5dfe35: consumer bead tracking the symptom; closes when this upstream bead lands + DDx bumps dep.
title: provider: strip Anthropic-specific fields (thinking) from non-Anthropic request bodies using DetectedFlavor() gate

description:
Implement the provider identity split described in docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-19-provider-routing-tool-output.md.

Provider now means the model source/counterparty, not an HTTP protocol shape. `openai`, `openrouter`, `lmstudio`, `omlx`, `ollama`, and `anthropic` are providers. The OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions/SSE shape is a shared implementation concern, not a provider identity.

Break backward compatibility where needed: remove `openai-compat` as a config provider type and remove runtime `flavor` provider behavior. URL/port heuristics may remain only as config-load inference for incomplete config. OpenRouter owns cost attribution. Local providers own endpoint pools and provider-specific discovery/limit logic.

This parent bead is decomposed by child implementation beads. Land against the provider conformance suite.
notes: 2026-04-19 design decision from user: do not preserve provider back-compat. Provider means a model source/counterparty, not an HTTP protocol shape: openai, openrouter, lmstudio, omlx, etc. Each provider owns its idiosyncrasies: auth/API key requirements, model listings/discovery, pricing/cost attribution, and local endpoint host:port configuration (e.g. lmstudio and omlx can require one or more host:port entries). Providers may share implementation elements such as an API shape or API client library, but that is a separate concern from provider identity. The OpenAI-compatible transport should be a shared SDK/client layer beneath those provider identities.
title: refactor: split 'openai' provider into openai-compat SDK + flavor-specific providers

acceptance:
1. internal/pty/cassette records version-1 manifests, timed input events, output.raw, timed output.jsonl raw chunk events, timed frame events, timed opaque service-event JSON, final metadata, optional quota data, and scrub reports through a documented API.
2. Every input, raw output chunk, frame, resize/signal, service-event, and final record includes monotonic global seq and monotonic t_ms; output.jsonl records byte offsets and lengths into output.raw with optional chunk digests and no inline bytes; manifest.timing records resolution_ms, clock policy, and replay default; version 1 defaults to resolution_ms=100 but accepts finer capture resolution without a schema bump.
3. Manifest v1 includes manifest.id, manifest.content_digest with sha256 over output.raw, and manifest.terminal.emulator {name, version}; readers expose these to assertion specs.
4. Recording is event-driven: timestamps are written on observed PTY/input/service transitions. Fixed-interval frame sampling may be supported only as an optional derived view, not as the authoritative recording model.
5. Event order is authoritative by global seq; same-t_ms events replay in seq order. Tests cover multiple events in one timestamp bucket.
6. A named CassetteTee-style interface lets harness adapters hand timed opaque service-event JSON and final metadata to internal/pty/cassette without any internal/pty package importing internal/harnesses or CONTRACT-003 typed-event decoders.
7. Replay supports realtime timing that honors recorded t_ms within one resolution tick, scaled timing, and collapsed timing under a virtual clock for deterministic CI.
8. Replay mode is credential-free, preserves input/output/frame/service-event ordering and raw output chunk boundaries, and exposes read-only event/frame/service-event iterators suitable for the follow-on internal/ptytest assertion framework.
9. Cassette replay supports read-only inspection inputs and never mutates recorded artifacts; readers reject unsupported newer major versions, missing required artifacts, missing required fields, unknown required feature flags, manifest/assertion ID mismatches, manifest/assertion digest mismatches, and emulator mismatches where frame assertions require a pinned emulator; readers may ignore unknown optional fields within the same major version; writers refuse to overwrite newer cassette schema versions.
10. Scrub rules and separate nondeterministic-output normalization are tested for environment allowlists, HOME/worktree path rewrites, tokens, account identifiers, UUID/request/session IDs, RFC3339/local timestamps, PIDs, elapsed durations, transient socket/file names, animation counters, and intentionally preserved fields.
11. Recorders compute t_ms from a monotonic elapsed clock such as time.Since(start) or injected test clock; tests cover a wall-clock jump without changing event ordering.
12. Authenticated record mode serializes per harness account and fails fast on lock contention rather than writing accepted artifacts from competing Claude/Codex record jobs.
13. Tests record and replay a deterministic synthetic terminal run through the direct PTY library and verify manifest fields, t_ms and seq ordering, realtime/scaled/collapsed playback behavior, raw output, output chunk offsets, frames, final status, schema-version handling, additive optional fields, unknown required fields, scrub/normalization reports, and parallel read-only replay safety.
14. The cassette package does not import internal/harnesses or contain Claude/Codex-specific quota/model logic; service events are stored as opaque JSON.
15. go test ./... passes.
description: Implement versioned cassette recording and playback as part of the ADR-002 direct PTY library, under internal/pty/cassette. The cassette layer consumes internal/pty/session and internal/pty/terminal, owns manifest/input/output/frame/service-event/final/quota/scrub artifacts, records monotonic event timestamps at a default 100ms resolution while allowing finer resolution_ms in version 1, and provides realtime, scaled, and collapsed replay. It remains free of harness-specific Claude/Codex policy.
title: harness-pty: implement PTY cassette recorder and playback library

acceptance: (1) Config schema supports endpoint-only blocks (no per-provider 'name' or 'model' fields required). (2) Service.Execute (not just cmd/agent routing_smart.go) probes /v1/models and normalizes the requested model against live catalogs case-insensitively, including suffix/prefix variants (Qwen3.5-27B-4bit ↔ qwen3.5-27b, qwen/qwen3.6 ↔ Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit). (3) Dead endpoints are skipped at probe time, not on the failing request. (4) When multiple live endpoints serve the same requested model, selection uses routing-metrics signals, not config ordering. (5) DDx's .ddx/config.yaml can express 'give me the best qwen3.6 you can find' without naming vidar-omlx or any specific provider. (6) Integration test: with two endpoints A (dead) and B (live, serves Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit), 'qwen3.6' resolves to B without hand-holding. (7) Regression: no existing acceptance test regresses.
description:
User-stated design direction (2026-04-21, DDx session). Current routing is built around named provider profiles (e.g. 'vidar-omlx', 'vidar-coder', 'bragi') each with a hard-coded model declaration. This breaks in multiple ugly ways and drove a zero-success drain of 32 test-migration beads on DDx:

Failure 1: 'cheap: qwen/qwen3.6' in .ddx/config.yaml routed to a DEAD vidar lmstudio endpoint (http://vidar:1234, 502 Bad Gateway) because the named profile declared its model as 'qwen3.5-27b' and the request fuzzy-matched on that string before any liveness check ran.

Failure 2: When failover hit vidar-omlx (http://vidar:1235), the request carried 'qwen3.5-27b' as the model string — but vidar-omlx actually serves 'Qwen3.5-27B-4bit' and 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit'. Every attempt 404'd with 'Model not found'.

Failure 3: routing_smart.go already has NormalizeModelID (internal/provider/openai/discovery.go) doing case-insensitive + suffix match against a discovered catalog, but it is only wired in cmd/agent routing_smart.go — NOT in the Service.Execute path that every consumer (DDx, etc.) actually hits.

Desired shape:

1. Config = endpoint pairs, not named profiles:
   endpoints:
     - {type: lmstudio, host: vidar, port: 1234}
     - {type: omlx, host: vidar, port: 1235}
     - {type: lmstudio, host: bragi, port: 1234}
     - {type: openrouter, api_key: ...}

2. On every dispatch, probe each endpoint's /v1/models with a short timeout. Skip endpoints that 502/timeout/empty.

3. Build a live {endpoint, model_id} matrix from the probe. The model_id is whatever the server actually serves — no per-provider name hardcoding.

4. Resolve the request (user asks for 'qwen3.6' or any case-variant) against the matrix:
   - case-insensitive contains / suffix / prefix match against every live model_id
   - if multiple endpoints match, tie-break on prior performance (latency, success rate, cost) from the routing-metrics store
   - if one matches, use it
   - if none match, explicit error ('no live endpoint offers a match for qwen3.6') + caller escalates

5. Kill the named-profile config-block lookups in the routing hot path. User-facing aliases (labels) are fine as shorthand but resolve through the same discovery flow.

6. NormalizeModelID's logic is the right primitive; extend and wire it into Service.Execute so every consumer benefits.

title: redesign: endpoint-first routing (kill named-provider profiles)

acceptance:
1. `ProviderEntry.CostUSDPer1kTokens float64` and `CostSource string` fields added with godoc.
2. `buildRoutingInputs` populates both fields for endpoint providers via model catalog lookup. Test seeds a catalog with 3 providers at different prices for the same model and asserts correct population.
3. Subscription harnesses (claude, codex, gemini) compute effective cost as a function of quota utilization per the curve in description. Unit test for each harness + each curve band.
4. Scoring tiebreak applies cost after base score, before locality. Test: 2 providers with identical score, different cost → lower cost wins. 2 providers with identical score, identical cost → locality decides (unchanged).
5. Unknown-cost providers are neutral in the tiebreak: test with mixed known and unknown costs asserts known-cost providers are not starved, unknown-cost providers are not demoted into last place.
6. `LocalCostUSDPer1kTokens` in `ServiceOptions` is honored when set; agent harness with local endpoints uses it. Unset = `CostSource: "unknown"`.
7. Near-quota demotion actually works: seed a claude harness at 92% quota, seed an openrouter provider with equivalent model at known cost → openrouter wins.
8. Cost and CostSource appear in `RouteCandidate` (via agent-53f38d95) so the decision trace shows which provider was picked and why.
9. Migration: existing tests pass unchanged (all current scoring cases assume zero-cost, which should still work).
10. Depends on agent-53f38d95 (decision trace) landing first so `CostSource` surfacing has a home.
description:
## Motivation

DDx consumer goal G3: "the selected provider should be the 'best' that meets whatever the requirements are — available, with quota available, **and lowest cost**." Today the routing engine has no cost dimension on `ProviderEntry`; scoring uses only quota + cooldown + perf + locality. When two providers serve the same model with different prices, the engine picks by name or score, not cost.

## Scope

### Data model

Add to `internal/routing.ProviderEntry`:

- `CostUSDPer1kTokens float64` — nil/zero if unknown.
- `CostSource string` — `"catalog" | "subscription" | "unknown" | "user-config"`. Surfaces in decision trace so operators know where the number came from.

Populate at `buildRoutingInputs` time:

- **Endpoint providers (openrouter, lmstudio, omlx, ollama, minimax, qwen, zai, openai):** look up in the existing model catalog (`internal/modelcatalog`) by resolved model ID. When the catalog has a price, use it. When absent, leave zero + `CostSource: "unknown"`.
- **Subscription harnesses (claude, codex, gemini):** compute an **effective** cost as a function of quota utilization:
  - At 0-70% quota used: effective cost = 0 (fixed-cost subscription, near-zero marginal).
  - At 70-90%: effective cost = low (encourage spreading load).
  - At 90-100%: effective cost = high (near-quota demotion).
  - Exact numbers in the bead acceptance; tunable via `ServiceOptions`.
  - `CostSource: "subscription"`.
- **`agent` harness (local via endpoints):** cost = electricity estimate from config (off by default — `CostSource: "unknown"` when not configured). Operators who want to model local cost set `ServiceOptions.LocalCostUSDPer1kTokens`.

### Scoring changes

`internal/routing/score.go`: add cost as a tiebreak dimension **after** eligibility, quota OK, and base score. Order becomes:
1. Eligible
2. Quota OK / healthy
3. Base score (profile policy, cooldowns, perf bias) — unchanged
4. **Cost (lower is better)** — new
5. Locality (local cost-class preferred)
6. Name

For unknown-cost providers (`CostSource: "unknown"`): treat as **neutral** in the cost tiebreak — neither preferred nor demoted, so they don't starve known-cost providers but aren't penalized into last place.

### Unknown-cost policy

A documented rule: when all viable candidates have `CostSource: "unknown"`, the cost tiebreak is skipped and locality/name decide. Test covers this.

## Subscription-quota pricing curve

Default curve (tunable):
- 0-70% used: effective $/1k tokens = 0
- 70-80%: 0.1× the pay-per-token rate for the equivalent model
- 80-90%: 0.3× pay-per-token
- 90-100%: 1.2× pay-per-token (demote below pay-per-token so routing drains the subscription gracefully)
notes:
`service_routing_test.go:289` (`TestResolveRouteNearQuotaClaudeDemotesBelowOpenRouter`) should be tightened so Claude and OpenRouter are otherwise tied, or should assert scores/cost ordering such that OpenRouter wins because Claude’s 92% subscription cost is higher. As written, `Profile: "standard"` uses local-first routing via `providerPreferenceForProfile` and the agent/openrouter path can win independently of the new cost curve.
Verification for AC9 needs a clean `go test ./...` run in an environment that permits `httptest` listeners. In this sandbox it failed with `socket: operation not permitted`, although `go test ./internal/routing` and the new targeted routing tests passed.
title: Cost-aware routing: CostUSDPer1kTokens on ProviderEntry + cost tiebreak + unknown-cost + subscription pricing

acceptance:
1. Boundary tests fail on forbidden cmd/agent dependencies such as internal/core, internal/provider/*, internal/session, internal/tool, internal/compaction, internal/harnesses, and internal/routing.
2. The stricter boundary test suite is part of the normal cmd/agent test path or CI gate.

description:
The existing boundary tests only ban agentcore.Run in cmd/agent/main.go and require DecodeServiceEvent on the execute path. They do not prevent the broader leak: the allowlist in cmd/agent/service_boundary_test.go still permits imports of internal/core, internal/session, internal/tool, internal/provider/openai, and other packages that should be forbidden once the service boundary is real. I confirmed that the current boundary suite passes while these leaks remain: go test ./cmd/agent -run "TestCLI(ServiceContractUsesTypedEventDecoder|MainDoesNotImportOrCallInternalCoreRun|InternalImportBoundaryAllowlist)" returns ok.

Tighten the executable boundary spec so regressions fail immediately. Replace the permissive allowlist with the minimal set that truly has no public replacement, add negative checks for forbidden internal packages and symbols, and wire the check into the normal test path/CI gate used for cmd/agent. If any CLI-visible behavior still needs a forbidden internal package, that is a blocker to fix in code or by extending the public contract first.

In-scope files:
- cmd/agent/service_boundary_test.go
- any helper test files or CI/test scripts needed to enforce the boundary
- contributor guidance if it needs to point at the executable boundary spec

Out of scope:
- The actual routing/session-log/provider refactors except as needed to make the stricter boundary tests pass
notes: Update dep target: was blocked on agent-9d120ece chain; now blocks on agent-21a521fc and agent-9c9cc191 (route-status redesign). The boundary test tightening still requires those CLI cleanups to land first.
title: Tighten cmd/agent boundary enforcement and CI checks

acceptance:
1. Child beads land such that cmd/agent is a consumer of the public DdxAgent service only for execution/routing/session-log concerns.
2. Routing, provider construction, native failover, and session-log persistence are service-owned and documented in CONTRACT-003/SD-002/SD-005/architecture.md.
3. Boundary tests and CI checks fail if cmd/agent reintroduces forbidden internal dependencies.
description:
The current boundary is only half-landed. cmd/agent now executes through service.Execute and decodes typed service events, but it still owns a parallel routing/provider-construction stack and still synthesizes private session-log lifecycle records. Concrete leaks called out in the architecture review: cmd/agent/provider_build.go rebuilds providers via internal/config + internal/core; cmd/agent/routing_provider.go and the route-selection logic in cmd/agent/main.go still construct route wrappers and candidate lists; cmd/agent/service_session_log_adapter.go writes internal session start/end records from public service events; cmd/agent/service_boundary_test.go allows internal imports that should be forbidden.

This epic drains that architecture debt and leaves one execution path: CLI as a first-party consumer of DdxAgent only, service as the owner of routing/provider construction/failover/session-log persistence, and provider adapters hidden behind the service boundary.

In-scope files: cmd/agent/*, service*.go, docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md, docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-002-standalone-cli.md, docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-provider-config.md, docs/helix/02-design/architecture.md, contributor guidance and boundary tests.

Out of scope for this epic: unrelated prompt/tool benchmarking work, provider-specific reasoning tuning, or harness-quality investigations.
title: Enforce the CLI -> service -> provider module boundary

2. Tests prove exact provider names still win over type fallback, default_provider is preferred when its type matches, stable ProviderNames order is used when default_provider type does not match, and no-match diagnostics list available provider names with types.
3. Routing/final metadata tests prove requested harness is preserved (for example `omlx`) while `RoutingActual.Provider` reports the resolved concrete provider name (for example `vidar-omlx`).
4. `go test ./internal/provider/openai ./internal/provider/omlx ./internal/sdk/openaicompat ./internal/harnesses` passes, preserving omlx/OpenAI-compatible streaming keep-alive comment-frame behavior.

description:
Surfaced while upgrading ddx (consumer) from ddx-agent v0.5.0 -> v0.6.0. After DDx migrates mechanical fields (Effort->Reasoning, SupportedEfforts->SupportedReasoning) and config migrates (thinking_level->reasoning, type: openai-compat -> concrete types), two agent-side gaps remain:

1. HTTP provider harnesses are declared but not dispatched by service.Execute.
   Repro: `ddx agent run --harness lmstudio --text ping --timeout 30s`
   Result: `error: harness "lmstudio" dispatch not yet wired in service.Execute`.
   The same class must work for `omlx`; omlx is a first-class OpenAI-compatible provider with existing streaming workarounds and tests that must be preserved.

2. Harness-to-provider resolution assumes provider is literally named after the harness.
   Example: tier routing resolves harness=lmstudio model=qwen3.5-27b, but native config lookup fails with `config: unknown provider "lmstudio"` when the config has concrete provider names such as bragi (type: lmstudio), vidar-omlx (type: omlx), or openrouter.

Expected: explicit HTTP-provider harnesses (`lmstudio`, `omlx`, `openrouter`) dispatch through the native provider path. If no literal provider name matches, resolution falls back to configured providers whose `type` equals the requested harness/provider, preferring default_provider when its type matches and otherwise using stable ProviderNames order. Failures list viable provider names and types.

Preserve existing omlx work: `internal/provider/omlx` must continue to use the OpenAI-compatible provider path and the SSE comment-frame workaround in `internal/sdk/openaicompat/sse_filter.go`; add or keep regression coverage with omlx-shaped keep-alive comment frames.

In-scope files:
- internal/harnesses/registry.go and registry tests
- service_execute.go
- service_native_provider.go
- service/service-level tests next to existing service tests
- internal/provider/omlx and OpenAI-compatible streaming regression tests as needed

Out-of-scope:
- Removing ProviderPreference or redesigning public profile routing; tracked separately by agent-285a05d2.
title: v0.6.0: lmstudio harness dispatch missing + native-config provider lookup assumes name parity

The prior discussion rejected a small incidental tmux dependency: either the project standardizes on tmux for the whole harness lifecycle, or it owns PTY lifecycle directly. If the reusable cassette player is generic enough to be its own project, define that boundary instead of burying it inside agent.

The decision must evaluate:
- direct PTY ownership using Go primitives and os/exec
- tmux as the session/process lifecycle manager

acceptance:
1. `internal/modelcatalog/catalog/models.yaml` (and equivalent config schema) contains first-class entries for `default`, `local`, `standard` with explicit `provider_preference` fields.
2. `providerPreferenceForProfile` in `service_routing.go` reads from the catalog for these + all other named profiles; the hardcoded switch statement is removed. Fallback path for unknown profile names returns a typed error, not a silent default.
3. `--profile local` against a registry with no local-endpoint candidates returns `ErrNoProfileCandidate` (or equivalent typed error) naming the profile and the missing capability. Never silently upgrades.
4. `--profile default` with a healthy endpoint serving a capable model produces a deterministic decision (documented tie-breaks applied in order).
5. `--profile local --harness claude` returns `ErrProfilePinConflict`. Test covers this and the inverse (`--profile smart --harness agent` when agent only has local endpoints).
6. DOC1 (profile catalog) published in `docs/` covering all profiles with: intent, provider preference, expected cost class, example use case.
7. DOC2 (override precedence) published, with worked examples from this bead's description.
8. Regression test in `internal/routing/` for each newly first-class profile: table-driven cases covering local-only success, local-only miss-then-error, default-then-tiebreak, standard-prefers-cost-balanced.
9. No existing test regression; all `TestResolveRoute_*` tests pass.
description:
## Motivation

DDx and other upstream consumers need `--profile default`, `--profile local`, `--profile standard` to be first-class profiles with documented, predictable rules. Today the engine treats `default` as an unnamed fall-through via `providerPreferenceForProfile(service_routing.go:368-377)` — the default case returns `ProviderPreferenceLocalFirst` — but there is no explicit catalog entry defining the profile's contract. `local` is not mapped at all (only `offline`/`air-gapped` map to `LocalOnly`). `standard` is unmapped and falls through identically to `default`.

A DDx consumer materializing `Profile: "default"` against an endpoint-only config gets unpredictable fallthrough behavior depending on which harnesses happen to be available. The profile catalog must define the contract.

## Scope

Add first-class profile entries in `internal/modelcatalog/catalog/models.yaml` for:

- `default` — balanced, prefer local endpoints when available, fall back to subscription harnesses, avoid pay-per-token unless specified. `ProviderPreferenceLocalFirst`.
- `local` — **hard** local-only constraint (same as `offline`). If no local-endpoint candidate is eligible, return typed error. `ProviderPreferenceLocalOnly`.
- `standard` — cost-balanced tier; prefer standard-capability endpoints. `ProviderPreferenceLocalFirst` but with cost-ceiling bias.

Retain existing `cheap`, `smart`, `fast`, `code-high`, `offline`, `air-gapped` entries unchanged.

Move the `providerPreferenceForProfile` decision out of hardcoded `service_routing.go` into the catalog so profile-to-preference mapping is data, not code.

## Override precedence (DOC2)

Publish the rule: **explicit pin wins within profile constraint; pin-violates-profile is a typed error, not a silent substitution.**

Worked examples included in docs:
- `--profile local --harness claude` → if claude is non-local (subscription harness), upstream returns `ErrProfilePinConflict` naming which side is incompatible.
- `--profile default --model gpt-5.4` → profile supplies preferences; explicit model overrides tier selection within those preferences.
- `--profile local --model opus-4.7` → if opus-4.7 is only servable by non-local harnesses, typed error.
execute-loop-last-detail: escalation exhausted: unsupported model "smart" for harness "codex"; supported models: gpt-5.4
title: Routing profiles: first-class default + local + standard with documented provider-preference rules

description:
First paired beadbench run for agent-vs-pi parity (2026-04-28) crashed the pi arm:

  panic: send on closed channel

  goroutine 189 [running]:
  github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent/internal/harnesses/pi.mirroredEvents.func1()
      .../internal/harnesses/pi/runner.go:338 +0x158
  created by github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent/internal/harnesses/pi.mirroredEvents
      .../internal/harnesses/pi/runner.go:332 +0xb4

Pi was paired with the openrouter qwen/qwen3.6-plus backing on the agent-beadbench-preflight task. Agent arm completed cleanly (different verify problem — see agent-9xxx); pi never got to the model.

The mirroredEvents goroutine is sending to a channel that the parent already closed — typical race between context cancellation / parent-return and the worker goroutine. Look for a missing select-on-ctx.Done() or a 'send only if not closed' pattern in runner.go:330-340.

## Reproducer

  python3 scripts/beadbench/run_beadbench.py \
    --arm pi-openrouter-qwen36plus \
    --task agent-beadbench-preflight

Crashes within ~20 seconds of start. Captured in benchmark-results/beadbench/run-20260428T032010Z-1595674/agent-beadbench-preflight__pi-openrouter-qwen36plus__r1/stderr.txt.

## Why P1

Blocks any agent-vs-pi parity measurement on the pi side. The whole point of agent-f43d1ed2 is the recurring parity tracker; pi can't participate until this is fixed.
title: fix(harness/pi): panic 'send on closed channel' in mirroredEvents

description:
Surfaced by DDx consumer review. DDx's cli/internal/agent/agent_catalog_shared.go reads ~/.config/agent/models.yaml directly and hard-codes surface translations like "agent.openai -> embedded-openai". That's a consumer baking in agent's catalog schema + internal surface naming. When agent renames a surface or restructures surface_policy (as v0.7.0 did), every consumer breaks.

With profile-owned placement policy (per agent-285a05d2), the catalog now carries placement, cost ceilings, failure policy, and reasoning defaults per profile/target. Consumers need to resolve a profile name to something routable — but they shouldn't be the ones reading YAML to do it.

## Proposal

Service exposes catalog + profile projection:

- Service.ListProfiles(ctx) -> []ProfileInfo  (name, description, deprecated alias-of, provenance)
- Service.ResolveProfile(ctx, name) -> ResolvedProfile (candidate surfaces + models + placement hint + cost ceiling + reasoning default + failure policy)
- Service.ProfileAliases(ctx) -> map[oldName]newName  (for deprecated aliases like the v0.6.0 harness-flavored preset removals — consumer gets a clear migration signal)

Consumers never touch ~/.config/agent/models.yaml, never know "agent.openai" surface naming.

## Consumer impact

Shrinks DDx ddx-e7254128 post-v0.7.0 catalog follow-up, ddx-ac5c7fdb (thin-consumer epic), ddx-bbb65768 (profile automation default), ddx-4b59b816 (cost-tiered routing lookups).
notes: Consolidated plan order 05. This can proceed independently of Execute event work, but should land before the larger health/quota status bead because consumers need profile and surface names from the service rather than from models.yaml. Current code evidence: internal/modelcatalog already resolves profiles, aliases, surface policies, cost ceilings, and reasoning defaults; the missing work is a public Service API projection and contract docs.
title: service: expose catalog profiles and surfaces via Service API (no consumer YAML parsing)

description:
FEAT-001-agent-loop.md acceptance criteria (AC-FEAT-001-01 through AC-FEAT-001-06) do not cover the three loop-detection behaviors shipped in v0.3.1:

1. ErrReasoningOverflow: stream aborted when model emits >32k chars of pure reasoning_content with no content/tool_call delta
2. ErrReasoningStall: stream aborted when only reasoning_content deltas arrive for >reasoningStallTimeout with no forward progress
3. ErrToolCallLoop: loop aborted when identical tool-call set repeats >=3 consecutive turns

Implementation: loop.go (toolCallLoopLimit, toolCallFingerprint, ErrToolCallLoop handling), stream_consume.go (reasoningByteLimit, stall timer, ErrReasoningOverflow/Stall), errors.go
Tests: TestRun_ToolCallLoopDetection, TestRun_ToolCallLoopCounterResetsOnDifferentCall (loop_test.go); TestConsumeStream_ReasoningOverflow, TestConsumeStream_ReasoningStall (stream_consume_test.go)

acceptance: Investigation captured: at least one of (a) a routing/catalog change that prevents Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit from being auto-selected for multi-file refactor work, (b) a fix to whatever in the agent's loop is causing the false-positive tool-call-repeat detection, OR (c) explicit confirmation in the catalog notes that this model isn't suitable for agentic refactor work. The seven beads cited in the description have a documented disposition (closed via different harness, deferred, or used as fixtures).
description:
Observed running ddx v0.6.2-alpha1 against agent v0.9.9 with worker:

    ddx work --harness omlx --model Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit --review-harness claude

Across 7 attempts on real SD-024 / ddx beads today (2026-04-25), the
model produced zero successful closes:

    Outcome                Count  Bead IDs
    execution_failed         4    ddx-0a33bc5f, ddx-915240dd,
                                  ddx-9ce6842a, ddx-dd7fce7e
    land_conflict            1    ddx-0b382233
    no_changes               2    ddx-d1d7ca17, ddx-ee2cd434

execution_failed cause (where logged): \"agent: identical tool calls
repeated, aborting loop\". Model gets stuck repeating the same tool
call until the loop's repetition guard aborts.

Token budgets per attempt range from 24K to 2.65M input tokens, with
durations 100s–6628s. The 2.65M-token attempt on ddx-0b382233
(land_conflict) is particularly notable — model spent ~41 minutes
producing a result that then couldn't merge.

The work being attempted is multi-file refactor (Go test-site
migration: rename type, move fields, update imports across ~10
files). This is the kind of work claude-opus consistently completes
in 7-15 minutes.

## What this isn't

- Not a routing/discovery problem. The model is reachable, dispatch
  works, the agent loop runs to completion.
- Not a context-window issue. 2.65M-input-token attempts consume
  large budget but don't fail on overflow — they fail on tool-loop
  detection or merge conflicts.
- Not a prompt-size issue (FEAT-022 evidence assembly is bounded).

## What this might be

- Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit lacks the agentic capability for multi-file
  refactors. May be a model-quality issue (size, quantization, base
  capability) rather than a harness/runtime bug.
- Tool-loop detection may be too eager and aborting valid work.
  Worth a separate investigation.
- Or qwen3.6 needs a different prompt template / harness adapter
  than what omlx currently uses.

## Why file on agent

Even if this is fundamentally model-quality, the agent module owns
harness-model routing decisions and the catalog metadata that informs
what model gets picked at each tier. If Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit
consistently fails on agent execute-bead workloads, the catalog
should either:
- Mark it as unsuitable for the standard tier
- Surface a capability constraint (e.g. NOT GOOD AT: multi-file refactor)
- Keep it but with a known low success rate that adaptive min-tier
  picks up automatically

## What to investigate

1. Reproduce: any agent execute-bead workload that touches >3 files
   with this model. Easy fixture: any of the 7 cited bead IDs in
   /Users/erik/Projects/ddx/.ddx/beads.jsonl.
2. Determine whether tool-loop detection is firing on legitimate
   repetition (false positive) or on actual model failure mode
   (true positive that's just exposing capability ceiling).
3. Decide on catalog metadata or routing policy to keep this model
   from being auto-selected for execute-bead workloads while
   keeping it available for ad-hoc ddx agent run invocations.
notes: Bumped P3 → P4. Per analysis in the bead description, this is a model-quality issue at Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit's size + quantization + agentic-multi-file-refactor combination, not a routing/discovery/context-window/prompt-size bug in this repo. Keeping open for tracking; no code change in this repo would fix Qwen's agentic capability. The one actionable angle that lives here — 'is tool-loop detection too eager?' — is a separate small bead that can be filed if/when investigation is justified by other models also tripping the guard.
title: omlx Qwen3.6-27B: zero successful closes across 7 attempts; tool-call loops + no_changes failures dominate

Depends on agent-bea748e7 (manifest arms must exist before doc references them).

## Sections to write

docs/research/AR-2026-04-26-agent-vs-pi-omlx-vidar-qwen36.md

### 1. Methodology

- Paired design (each task runs on BOTH agent and pi arms with same omlx-vidar Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit backing).
- Reviewer pinned at codex/gpt-5.5 (cannot be claude — confound).
- Sample size N>=8 paired tasks; target N=12.
- Win condition: success rate >= pi's; cost-per-success <= 1.2x pi's.
- Tiebreaker: prefer native agent within 5% margin (lower indirection).

description:
First-run cold model load on vidar burns ~7 minutes (turns 1-2 at 15-16 tps, ~200s each, while ~27GB Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit weights load into VRAM). On a 15-min sub-run budget that's ~47% gone before any useful work.

Mitigation: send a tiny prompt (e.g. 'pong' completion at max_tokens=1) to each provider+model pair in the manifest during preflight, before the timed sub-runs start. The model then stays resident across sub-runs in the same beadbench invocation.

Forensic source: run-20260429T003337Z-97454/agent-nav-tools__agent-omlx-vidar-qwen36__r1 — turn-1 latency 200.6s for 3209 tokens (16 tps), turn-2 latency 226.8s (15 tps), turn-3 latency 11.5s (302 tps effective). The 12.5x speedup at turn 3 confirms the bottleneck is one-time load, not per-request caching.
title: beadbench: pre-warm vidar MLX before paired runs

acceptance:
1. DOC1 published in `docs/routing/profiles.md` (or equivalent). All profiles in description listed with the required sections.
2. DOC2 published in `docs/routing/override-precedence.md`. Precedence rule stated numerically; at least 8 worked examples including the contradictory cases.
3. DOC3 published in `docs/routing/best-provider.md`. Formal definition + per-profile overrides documented.
4. Each document is linked from the top-level README or an equivalent routing index page.
5. Docs are verified against code: a small test script (or doc-check) asserts every profile in the catalog has a DOC1 entry and every named profile in DOC1 has a catalog entry. No drift.
6. DDx's CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md updated (in its own PR against the DDx repo, linked in this bead's closing notes) to point at DOC1/2/3 rather than duplicate.

closing-notes: DDx companion PR for CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md routing links: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/pull/51
description:
## Motivation

DDx consumer goals G2 and G3 require documented, predictable behavior. Today the routing rules are distributed across code (`service_routing.go`, `engine.go`, `gating.go`, `score.go`), registry (`internal/harnesses/registry.go`), and catalog (`internal/modelcatalog/`). Consumers have no single source of truth. CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md in DDx will link to these docs rather than duplicate them.

## Scope

Three docs in `docs/` (or equivalent) of the ddx-agent repo:

### DOC1 — Profile catalog

Complete list of named profiles with, for each:
- Name
- Intent (one sentence)
- `ProviderPreference` (local-first / local-only / subscription-first)
- Expected cost class
- Example use case
- Any hard constraints (e.g., `local` rejects non-local candidates)

Profiles covered at minimum after agent-191a74f9 lands: `default`, `standard`, `local`, `cheap`, `smart`, `fast`, `code-high`, `offline`, `air-gapped`.

### DOC2 — Override precedence

Precedence rule:
1. Explicit `Harness` pin wins within its allow-list.
2. Explicit `Model` pin wins within the chosen harness's allow-list.
3. Explicit `Provider` pin wins within the chosen harness-model pair.
4. Profile supplies preferences for any dimension not pinned.
5. Pin-violates-profile-hard-constraint = typed error (`ErrProfilePinConflict` from agent-dfabb10b), not silent substitution.

Include worked examples for each combination, including the contradictory cases (`--profile local --harness claude`, `--profile smart --harness agent` with only local endpoints).

### DOC3 — Best-provider contract

Formal definition of "best":
1. Available (harness binary present, provider endpoint reachable).
2. Quota OK (quota available for quota-tracked harnesses; unconstrained for pay-per-token).
3. Lowest cost among candidates that meet (1) and (2). Unknown cost = neutral in the tiebreak.
4. Locality tiebreak (local cost-class preferred when cost is equal or all unknown).
5. Deterministic by name when all else is equal.

Include per-profile override of the ordering (e.g., `local` adds a hard constraint before step 1; `smart` may reorder cost vs capability).

## Consumers

- DDx `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` link to DOC1/2/3 directly.
- DDx `docs/helix/02-design/` design notes reference by stable URL.
- DDx CLI `--help` for `--profile` / `--harness` / `--model` points at DOC2 for precedence.
notes:
DDx companion PR for CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md routing links: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/pull/51

REVIEW:BLOCK

docs/routing/best-provider.md:22 — DOC3 defines profile score before cost, making cost only a tie-break among equal scores. AC3 requires lowest cost among available/quota-OK candidates before locality/name tie-breaks, with profile-specific reorderings documented as overrides.
Bead closing evidence / DDx repo PR — AC6 requires DDx’s CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md to be updated in its own PR and linked in closing notes, but the supplied diff contains no external PR link or closing-note evidence.
title: Routing documentation: profile catalog (DOC1), override precedence (DOC2), best-provider contract (DOC3)

acceptance: rg -n 'owns passive failover|tries the next eligible|same-profile failover|performs failover|failover internally|may trigger same-profile failover' docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-005-smart-routing-replaces-model-routes.md docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-provider-config.md docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md returns no agent-owned retry/failover claims; docs state caller owns all retry dispatches
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-005-smart-routing-replaces-model-routes.md:119 and docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-provider-config.md:57
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The routing docs still assign passive failover/retry behavior to the agent service: ADR-005 says Execute tries the next eligible candidate, and SD-005 says embedded ddx-agent owns passive failover. The clarified contract is stricter: agent should report provider/availability/transport failure such as a provider unexpectedly stopping bytes, but it should not attempt retries. The caller owns all retry decisions, including whether to try another candidate under the same profile.
Suggested fix: Rewrite ADR-005, SD-005, and CONTRACT-003 so agent returns a complete ordered candidate trace plus the attempted candidate failure, but never dispatches candidate 2 automatically. Replace retry/failover wording with caller-owned retry language and expose enough route facts for the caller to retry deliberately.
notes: Incorporated in docs: agent dispatches exactly one candidate and reports route outcome; all retry/follow-up dispatch is caller-owned. Verified prohibited agent-owned failover phrases are absent from ADR-005, SD-005, and CONTRACT-003.
title: correctness: remove agent-owned routing retries

acceptance: go test -race ./... passes; Tool interface or wrapper gains a Parallel bool indicator; read/glob/grep/ls are marked Parallel=true; write/edit/patch/bash/task are Parallel=false; TestRun_ParallelToolExecution exists in loop_test.go and verifies concurrent dispatch of parallel-flagged tools; Result.ToolCalls preserves original provider-returned order
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<governing>SD-001 §D4 — tools implement the Tool interface with Name(), Description(), Schema(), Execute(). SD-001 §Business Rules rule 2 — "Tool execution is sequential" (this bead changes that rule for Parallel-flagged tools). FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-02 — tool calls execute in provider order and record each call.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently loop.go executes all tool calls sequentially regardless of whether they could safely run in parallel. Codex uses an explicit per-tool parallel flag to run independent tools concurrently, reducing wall time.

What to change:

1. agent.go (Tool interface or Tool struct): Add Parallel bool field to the Tool interface or to a ToolOptions/ToolMeta struct embedded in Tool. The simplest approach: add a Parallel() bool method to the Tool interface, or add a Parallel bool to a concrete ToolDef wrapper. Choose whichever fits the existing pattern best.

2. tool/ built-ins: Mark read, glob, grep, ls as Parallel: true (read-only, safe to parallelize). Mark write, edit, patch, bash, task as Parallel: false (stateful/side-effects, must serialize).

3. loop.go tool execution: When a provider response contains multiple tool calls, partition them into parallel and serial groups. Execute all consecutive parallel tools as a group using goroutines + errgroup (or sync.WaitGroup). Execute serial tools one at a time. Preserve the original order in Result.ToolCalls (sort by provider-returned order, not completion order).

4. Tests: Add TestRun_ParallelToolExecution in loop_test.go verifying parallel-flagged tools execute concurrently (use a mock provider that returns 3 parallel tool calls in one response; use sync.WaitGroup or channel timing to confirm concurrent dispatch). go test -race ./... must pass.

Governing specs: SD-001, FEAT-001, FEAT-002.
title: feat: per-tool Parallel flag with concurrent tool execution

acceptance:
- [ ] After the fix, a session that performs zero actual compactions emits zero compaction.start/end pairs at default verbosity (or at most one compaction.skip per turn)
- [ ] When compaction does perform real work (messages_after < messages_before OR token count drops), events are still emitted with the same shape
- [ ] Regression test asserts no-op compactions do not emit events at default verbosity
description:
The compaction package emits a compaction.start + compaction.end event pair around every turn (pre-turn check + post-turn check), even when no compaction is performed. Observed on 2026-04-18 in a DDx ddx work drain on bragi qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b at session log .ddx/executions/20260418T191746-684f612a/embedded/agent-1776539866960442383.jsonl:

  {"type":"compaction.start","data":{"messages_before":1}}
  {"type":"compaction.end","data":{"messages_after":1,"messages_before":1,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
  {"type":"compaction.start","data":{"messages_before":6,"mid_turn":true}}
  {"type":"compaction.end","data":{"messages_after":6,"messages_before":6,"mid_turn":true,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
  ... and so on every turn

When no_compaction:true and messages_before == messages_after, the compactor was asked, looked at the input, and decided nothing needed compacting. That is not interesting telemetry.

Real-world impact:
  - Misleads operators (the DDx team initially diagnosed cheap-tier slowness as compaction-thrash; the actual cause was per-turn latency)
  - Inflates session-log size with hundreds of no-op event pairs
  - Makes it harder to spot ACTUAL compactions when scanning the log

Fix options:
  1. Suppress the start+end pair entirely when the compactor would no-op. Only log when work was actually performed.
  2. Emit a single compaction.skip event instead of the pair, with a reason field.
  3. Demote no-op events to a low-verbosity tier behind a debug flag.

Cross-ref: filed downstream on DDx tracker as ddx-18c7de20 and is being closed in favor of this upstream bead.
notes: Superseded by agent-fbc6b277 (in the LLM-routing-consolidation epic agent-a770e781). The suppression logic was already in place in loop.go; agent-fbc6b277 added a regression test guarding it. Closing this bead as superseded.
title: compaction: suppress no-op start/end telemetry pair when nothing was compacted

description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>

Currently tool/bash.go returns raw combined output as the tool result string. Codex normalizes shell output into a human-readable structured format before inserting it into the prompt, improving model comprehension of tool results.

What to change in tool/bash.go:

acceptance:
1. service_status_test.go::TestListHarnesses_GeminiAccountAndUsageWindows runs deterministically regardless of UTC time-of-day. Verified by running with t.Setenv-injected fake clock or equivalent fixture clock that anchors 'now' to a fixed time.
2. The test asserts len(UsageWindows) == 3 with all three names present.
3. go test . -run TestListHarnesses_GeminiAccountAndUsageWindows -count=10 passes 10/10 in a CI-like loop without time manipulation.
4. go test ./... -count=1 passes.
description:
Pre-existing test failure surfaced when running the suite just after UTC midnight. Confirmed at HEAD~5 (commit f6e7487) so the bug predates v0.9.10 caching work and the ADR-006 chain.

## Symptom

go test . -run TestListHarnesses_GeminiAccountAndUsageWindows fails with len(geminiInfo.UsageWindows) == 2 instead of expected 3. The 'today' window is missing; only '7d' and '30d' come back.

## Root cause

service_status_test.go:165 sets up the test fixture with:
    start := time.Now().UTC().Add(-time.Hour)

When the test runs between 00:00 and 01:00 UTC, 'start' lands in YESTERDAY's UTC date. service.geminiUsageWindows() iterates ['today', '7d', '30d'] and the 'today' aggregation finds no records dated today UTC, so harnessUsageWindow returns nil and the slice ends up with only 2 entries.

## Fix options

1. Use a fixed time injection: pass a deterministic 'now' into the service rather than time.Now(). The aggregation path already accepts opts.Now in sessionusage.UsageOptions; thread the same fixture clock through ListHarnesses so the test is independent of wall-clock time.
2. Move the fixture's start time forward enough to clear midnight: start := time.Now().UTC().Truncate(24*time.Hour).Add(12*time.Hour) — anchor at midday. Less robust if the test runs across midnight; option 1 is cleaner.
3. Loosen the assertion to accept 2 windows when 'start' is in yesterday's UTC date. Test masks the flake but doesn't fix it.

Option 1 is the right fix.

## Why not just close

Bead surfaces an actual midnight-window bug worth fixing properly so the suite is deterministic regardless of wall-clock UTC time. Low priority because it's a test-only flake, but real.
title: test: TestListHarnesses_GeminiAccountAndUsageWindows fails near UTC midnight (today window empty)

acceptance:
1. service_*_test.go tests calling agent.New() with default options either set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a t.TempDir() or pass an explicit ServiceConfig that doesn't depend on the user's live config.
2. 'go test ./...' from a verify-worktree at base_rev 8925d4b (or any commit predating lucebox/vllm) passes regardless of what the runtime user has in ~/.config/agent/config.yaml.
description:
Several service_*_test.go tests call agent.New(...) with default options. agent.New defaults to loading the user's \$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/agent/config.yaml. Any provider type added to the user's config that the agent module under test doesn't recognize causes those tests to fail with 'unknown provider type'.

Today's beadbench preflight run (agent-beadbench-preflight against agent-openrouter-qwen36plus, 2026-04-28) hit this: the bead executes correctly through the model loop, but the verify gate runs 'go test ./...' against the bead's verify-worktree at base_rev 8925d4b (pre-lucebox). That older code rejects 'type: lucebox' / 'type: vllm' from the user's live config.

Failing tests captured (sample):

  service_providers_test.go:680 TestNewWaitsBrieflyForInvalidQuotaRefresh
  service_providers_test.go:721 TestNewStartupQuotaRefreshContinuesAfterTimeout
  service_providers_test.go:765 TestPrimaryQuotaRefreshWorkerRefreshesOnTimer
  service_validation_test.go:15 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessModelBeforeRun
  service_validation_test.go:43 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessReasoningBeforeRun
  service_harness_dispatch_test.go:41,90,119 TestExecute_Dispatches*
  service_profiles_test.go:13,61,112 TestServiceProfiles_*

All same root cause: agent.New() loads the live user config without test isolation.

## Fix paths

1. Tests that don't actually need the user's config should pass an explicit ServiceConfig stub (no providers, or a known shape).
2. Or, set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a temp dir in test setup so agent.New() loads an empty config.
3. Or, add an option to agent.New that skips XDG lookup.

Pick whichever is most consistent with how other test-isolation patterns work in this repo.

## Why P2

Doesn't block normal CI (CI clean container has no user config). Does affect any verify-gate run on a base revision predating a provider-type addition — a bead correctness signal we care about during parity measurements.
title: test(service): tests calling agent.New() leak user config — break verify against base revisions

acceptance: RouteRequest.ProviderPreference removed from public API. Profiles (named multi-axis policy bundles) documented in CONTRACT-003 as the routing knob. models.yaml surface_policy carries placement + cost + failure policy. Consumer migration note published.
description:
Surfaced while migrating ddx (consumer) from ddx-agent v0.5.0 → v0.6.0 (see DDx tracker ddx-4535f466; sibling agent bead agent-117a0868).

## Argument

v0.6.0 added `ProviderPreference` (`LocalFirst` / `SubscriptionFirst` / `LocalOnly` / `SubscriptionOnly`) to `RouteRequest` as the consumer-facing placement knob. This sits in an uncanny valley:

- It's **too abstract to be a real choice** — a caller who cares enough about placement to set it usually has more specific intent ("only local because I'm offline", "cloud because this one task is worth $2", "openrouter-haiku for this batch because I know the shape"). The 4-value enum can't express any of that.

- It's **too specific to delegate** — a caller who doesn't want to think about placement shouldn't have to pick one of four values at every call site. The right default depends on tier, cost budget, connectivity, and user policy — none of which `ProviderPreference` sees.

- Every consumer that plumbs it through ends up re-deriving the same tier→preference translation (cheap → LocalFirst, smart → SubscriptionFirst), which is (a) duplicated across consumers, (b) guaranteed to drift, and (c) not tunable without a code change.

## The richer abstraction that already almost exists: profiles

A profile is a named bundle of multi-axis policy. Examples of what profiles can express that `ProviderPreference` can't:

- `cheap`: prefer local; fall back to openrouter-haiku; never opus; stall-kill aggressively; cap reasoning at 2k tokens
- `offline`: local-only; fail loud if no local provider; no network calls at all
- `smart-but-cheap`: subscription-first but only models under $5/Mtok input
- `air-gapped`: local-only + disable telemetry + reject any http provider
- `code-high`: any provider, coding-tuned models, reasoning=high, prefer large context

`ProviderPreference` reduces all of that to one axis. A profile is a data structure, and data structures compose.

v0.6.0's `models.yaml` already has `surface_policy` per target carrying `effort_default` and `reasoning_default`. Extending that to carry placement order, cost ceilings, and failure policy is an additive change; the shape is already there.

## Proposal

1. **Remove `RouteRequest.ProviderPreference`** from the public service surface (v0.7.0 breaking change; acceptable now because adoption is low and we're already mid-migration).
2. **Reinstate profiles as the consumer-facing routing knob.** `RouteRequest.Profile` (or let `ModelRef` carry a profile name — today `ModelRef` already accepts tier names like `cheap`/`standard`/`smart`, so this may be a simple semantic consolidation, not a new field).
3. **Move placement policy into profile/catalog definitions.** Each profile/target declares its own candidate providers, candidate models, placement order, reasoning defaults, cost ceilings, and failure policy. Users retune by editing one file (`models.yaml` or user override).
4. **Pinpoint control stays as an escape hatch.** Callers that already know the exact provider + model pass `Provider` + `Model` directly and bypass the profile machinery entirely. That's the "if I'm going to micromanage I'll just set the provider" path — preserved, not denied.
5. **No per-call-site policy expression.** Consumers that don't know placement don't have to decide it. The profile does.

## Why this matters for the consumer side

DDx's ddx-4535f466 Track B was originally "audit every `ResolveRoute` call site and add the right `ProviderPreference`." If this proposal lands, that track collapses to zero: callers either use a profile (and the agent decides placement) or pin Provider+Model (and they own placement). No middle layer for DDx to translate.

## Acceptance

1. `RouteRequest.ProviderPreference` removed from the public surface; `ProviderPreference` constants no longer exported.
2. Profiles (named, multi-axis policy bundles) are the documented public routing knob. CONTRACT-003 updated.
3. `surface_policy` in `models.yaml` carries placement order + cost ceilings + failure policy, not just reasoning defaults.
4. A consumer wanting "local-first cheap" sets `Profile: "cheap"` and the catalog's cheap profile expresses "prefer local, fall back to openrouter-haiku". A consumer wanting pinpoint control sets `Provider` + `Model` and bypasses the profile machinery.
5. Migration note in CHANGELOG for any v0.6.0 consumer that started using `ProviderPreference`.
title: RouteRequest.ProviderPreference is the uncanny valley — reinstate profiles as the public routing knob

- force interrupted/partial streaming responses in each provider package and prove partial response state is preserved while an error is surfaced

title: docs: remove named-provider and named-profile routing frame
